# LLM Anti-Patterns — Aura to LWC Migration

Common mistakes AI coding assistants make when generating or advising on Aura-to-LWC migration.
These patterns help the consuming agent self-check its own output.

## Anti-Pattern 1: Using $A in LWC JavaScript

**What the LLM generates:** Port Aura controller code into an LWC `.js` file with `$A` calls intact:
```javascript
// Wrong — generated by LLM copying Aura controller verbatim
connectedCallback() {
    var action = $A.get("c.getAccountData");
    action.setParams({ accountId: this.recordId });
    $A.enqueueAction(action);
}
```

**Why it happens:** LLMs trained on large corpora of Salesforce code see both Aura and LWC examples. When asked to "convert" a component, they may preserve `$A` calls and reframe them inside LWC lifecycle hooks, pattern-matching on the structural similarity without understanding the framework boundary.

**Correct pattern:**
```javascript
// Correct — imperative Apex in LWC
import { LightningElement, api, track } from 'lwc';
import getAccountData from '@salesforce/apex/AccountController.getAccountData';

export default class AccountComponent extends LightningElement {
    @api recordId;
    @track accountData;

    connectedCallback() {
        getAccountData({ accountId: this.recordId })
            .then(result => { this.accountData = result; })
            .catch(error => { console.error(error); });
    }
}
```

**Detection hint:** Search generated code for `$A\.` — any match in an LWC `.js` file is a definite error.

---

## Anti-Pattern 2: Generating an Aura Application Event Handler in LWC

**What the LLM generates:** An LWC component with an Aura application event handler registration:
```javascript
// Wrong — LWC cannot receive Aura application events
connectedCallback() {
    // Attempt to register for Aura app event from LWC
    this.template.addEventListener('c:myAppEvent', this.handleMyEvent.bind(this));
}
```
Or worse, generating markup like:
```html
<!-- Wrong — not valid LWC syntax -->
<aura:handler event="c:MyAppEvent" action="{handleMyEvent}"/>
```

**Why it happens:** The LLM knows Aura application events exist and attempts to reconcile them with LWC's `addEventListener` pattern, producing code that looks plausible but will never fire.

**Correct pattern:**
```javascript
// Correct — subscribe to LMS channel instead
import { subscribe, MessageContext, unsubscribe } from 'lightning/messageService';
import MY_CHANNEL from '@salesforce/messageChannel/MyChannel__c';
import { wire } from 'lwc';

// In the class:
@wire(MessageContext) messageContext;

connectedCallback() {
    this.subscription = subscribe(this.messageContext, MY_CHANNEL, (msg) => {
        this.handleMessage(msg);
    });
}
disconnectedCallback() { unsubscribe(this.subscription); }
```

**Detection hint:** Look for `addEventListener` with a namespaced event name like `c:` or `namespace:` in LWC `.js` files. Also look for `<aura:handler` tags in `.html` files.

---

## Anti-Pattern 3: Embedding Aura Component Tags Directly in LWC Template

**What the LLM generates:** An LWC `.html` template that includes an Aura component tag as a child:
```html
<!-- Wrong — Aura components cannot be used as children in LWC templates -->
<template>
    <div class="container">
        <c-legacy-aura-sidebar record-id={recordId}></c-legacy-aura-sidebar>
    </div>
</template>
```

**Why it happens:** The LLM knows that LWC uses kebab-case component tags and that `c-` prefix identifies custom components, so it pattern-matches the Aura component name to a valid-looking LWC import. The component deploys without error but the Aura child renders as an empty DOM element.

**Correct pattern:**

If the Aura component must be retained during migration, create an Aura wrapper that contains it and communicate via LMS. If this is a new LWC component, the child should already be a fully migrated LWC component, imported normally:
```javascript
import LegacyAuraSidebar from 'c/legacyAuraSidebar'; // only valid if this IS already an LWC
```
The migration checklist must confirm the child is LWC before treating it as an LWC import.

**Detection hint:** Cross-reference the component tag names in an LWC `.html` template against the `aura/` directory. Any tag referencing a known Aura component name that has not yet been migrated is a likely error.

---

## Anti-Pattern 4: Generating event.getParam() in LWC Event Handlers

**What the LLM generates:** An LWC event handler that uses Aura event parameter accessor APIs:
```javascript
// Wrong — event.getParam does not exist in LWC CustomEvent
handleAccountSelected(event) {
    const recordId = event.getParam('recordId');    // TypeError at runtime
    const data = event.getParams();                  // TypeError at runtime
}
```

**Why it happens:** The LLM has seen many Aura controller examples using `event.getParam()` and `event.getParams()`. When generating an LWC handler for a "converted" event, it carries over the accessor pattern without adjusting for the DOM standard `CustomEvent` API.

**Correct pattern:**
```javascript
// Correct — access CustomEvent payload via event.detail
handleAccountSelected(event) {
    const recordId = event.detail.recordId;
    const data = event.detail;
}
```

**Detection hint:** Search generated `.js` files for `event\.getParam\(` or `event\.getParams\(` — both are Aura-only APIs and are errors in any LWC context.

---

## Anti-Pattern 5: Assuming NavigationMixin is a Drop-In for force:navigate* Events

**What the LLM generates:** A direct translation that uses `NavigationMixin.Navigate` with an incorrect page reference type, or that fires the navigation outside the correct `this[NavigationMixin.Navigate]` call syntax:
```javascript
// Wrong — incorrect NavigationMixin usage patterns

// Mistake 1: calling as a method rather than computed property
this.NavigationMixin.Navigate({ type: 'standard__recordPage', ... });

// Mistake 2: using force:navigateToSObject event type string as NavigationMixin type
this[NavigationMixin.Navigate]({ type: 'force:navigateToSObject', ... }); // invalid type string

// Mistake 3: omitting NavigationMixin from the class declaration
export default class MyComponent extends LightningElement { // forgot NavigationMixin
    navigate() {
        this[NavigationMixin.Navigate](...); // NavigationMixin.Navigate is undefined
    }
}
```

**Why it happens:** The `this[NavigationMixin.Navigate]` syntax using a computed property key is unusual in JavaScript and easy to mis-generate. LLMs also pattern-match on the Aura event type strings and sometimes carry them into the LWC page reference `type` field.

**Correct pattern:**
```javascript
import { LightningElement } from 'lwc';
import { NavigationMixin } from 'lightning/navigation';

// Class must extend NavigationMixin(LightningElement)
export default class CaseNavigator extends NavigationMixin(LightningElement) {
    navigateToCase(caseId) {
        // type string must be a valid PageReference type, not an Aura event name
        this[NavigationMixin.Navigate]({
            type: 'standard__recordPage',
            attributes: {
                recordId: caseId,
                objectApiName: 'Case',
                actionName: 'view'
            }
        });
    }
}
```

**Detection hint:** Verify the class declaration extends `NavigationMixin(LightningElement)` — not just `LightningElement`. Check that the `type` field value is a valid `PageReference` type string (`standard__recordPage`, `standard__objectPage`, `standard__webPage`, `standard__namedPage`, `standard__app`) and not an Aura event name.

---

## Anti-Pattern 6: Using camelCase in CustomEvent Names

**What the LLM generates:** A CustomEvent dispatched with a camelCase name, matched with a camelCase `on*` handler in the template:
```javascript
// Wrong — camelCase event name in CustomEvent constructor
this.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('accountSelected', { detail: { id: this.recordId } }));
```
```html
<!-- Wrong — camelCase on* attribute in template -->
<c-account-tile onaccountSelected={handleAccountSelected}></c-account-tile>
```

**Why it happens:** Aura component event names were often camelCase (e.g., `accountSelected`). LLMs carry this naming convention into LWC CustomEvent generation. LWC event names in template attributes must be all lowercase; the `on` + camelCase combination is a common source of silent handler failures.

**Correct pattern:**
```javascript
// Correct — all lowercase event name
this.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('accountselected', { detail: { id: this.recordId } }));
```
```html
<!-- Correct — all lowercase on* attribute -->
<c-account-tile onaccountselected={handleAccountSelected}></c-account-tile>
```

**Detection hint:** Search for `new CustomEvent('` followed by a name containing uppercase letters. All CustomEvent constructor name strings in LWC should be lowercase (hyphens allowed for compound names like `account-selected`, which maps to `onaccount-selected` in the template).
